did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated a scalable containerized deep learning system for automated skin cancer detection. It utilized Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically evaluating CNN, VGG19, and ResNet50 architectures, with ResNet50 achieving 92.33% accuracy on the ISIC dataset for binary classification of benign versus malignant skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing and evaluating a Scalable Containerized Deep Learning System (CDLS) for automated skin cancer detection. It details the methodology (using CNN, VGG19, and ResNet50 on the ISIC dataset), implementation (Docker, Kubernetes, Prometheus), and reports new results (92.33% accuracy for ResNet50). Although published in a conference proceedings (AIxMHC), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Scalable Containerized Deep Learning System for Automated Skin Cancer Detection
journal: 2025 Second International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Medicine, Health and Care (AIxMHC)
brief_summary: This study addresses the need for scalable and reliable automated skin cancer detection by developing a Containerized Deep Learning System (CDLS). The researchers utilized the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset to train and evaluate three Convolutional Neural Network architectures: CNN, VGG19, and ResNet50 for binary classification of benign versus malignant lesions. To enhance model robustness and generalization, the system employed data augmentation and transfer learning techniques, with ResNet50 achieving the highest classification accuracy of 92.33%. The trained model was deployed using Docker and Kubernetes to ensure platform-independent scalability and fault tolerance. Additionally, Prometheus was integrated for real-time monitoring of critical performance metrics such as CPU usage and memory consumption. The results demonstrate that the proposed CDLS provides a practical, scalable, and transparent approach for deploying intelligent skin cancer detection systems in clinical environments.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task using the ISIC dataset. Labelled as 0 in the dataset D = {(xi, yi)} where yi ∈ {0, 1}.

One of the two classes in the binary classification task using the ISIC dataset. Labelled as 1 in the dataset D = {(xi, yi)} where yi ∈ {0, 1}. The paper notes melanoma is the most aggressive subtype of skin cancer in the introduction, but the experiment classifies broadly as malignant vs benign.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign (0) or malignant (1) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'automated skin cancer classification' using the ISIC dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant). The output layer uses sigmoid activation to produce a single probability score for binary classification, and the primary metric reported is classification accuracy (92.33% for ResNet50).

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin cancer images obtained from the ISIC archive, used for binary classification of benign versus malignant lesions. Images were resized to 224x224 pixels and normalized.

use.training: Used to train the CNN, VGG19, and ResNet50 models with data augmentation applied only to this split.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the trained models (CNN, VGG19, ResNet50).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy and loss, and compared qualitatively with other studies.

ISIC Archive.notes: ResNet-50 achieved the highest classification accuracy of 92.33% on the test set. The paper also compares these results to related works: VGG-13 (89.57% accuracy) and ResNet152 (90.04% accuracy) on the ISIC dataset. System monitoring metrics for the deployed ResNet-50 model included average CPU load of 9.37%, memory utilization of 29%, and storage utilization of 18.35%.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet; convolutional layers retained for feature extraction, with deeper layers adjusted during fine-tuning

custom_layers: Two additional Conv2D layers with 64 filters each
Flattening layer producing a 256-dimensional feature vector
Sequence of fully connected dense layers with dimensions: 512, 256, 128, 64, 32, and 16 units
architecture_details.activations: ReLU for dense layers; Sigmoid for final output layer

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named (minimized binary cross-entropy loss)
training_details.data_augmentation: Horizontal flipping, random rotations (up to 45 degrees), zooming (up to 20%), width and height shifts
training_details.hardware: Intel Core i7 CPU, 32 GB RAM, NVIDIA RTX 3060 GPU

classification_or_output_head.structure: Final dense layer with sigmoid activation producing a single probability score in [0, 1]

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: 25.1 million total parameters (25,058,058 trainable weights and 53,120 frozen parameters)

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization to the [0, 1] range using Min-Max scaling
Data augmentation applied only to the training set, including: horizontal flipping, random rotations (up to 45 degrees), zooming (up to 20%), and width/height shifts

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied dynamically during training to improve model robustness and generalization.
Applied with a maximum range of up to 45 degrees.
Applied with a maximum range of up to 20%.
Applied dynamically during training to introduce spatial variability.

conclusions: The proposed Scalable Containerized Deep Learning System (CDLS) effectively addresses limitations in scalability, deployment flexibility, and real-time monitoring for automated skin cancer detection.

Among the evaluated architectures (CNN, VGG19, and ResNet50), ResNet50 achieved the highest classification accuracy of 92.33% on the ISIC dataset for binary classification of benign versus malignant lesions.

ResNet50 demonstrated superior performance compared to related works using VGG-13 (89.57% accuracy) and ResNet152 (90.04% accuracy), confirming the effectiveness of residual learning for extracting deep features in medical image analysis.
The CNN model exhibited unstable learning behavior with fluctuating loss values, while VGG-19 showed early saturation, resulting in lower test accuracies of 82.50% and 89.39%, respectively.
Containerization using Docker and Kubernetes provides platform-independent scalability, fault tolerance, and reproducible deployment for deep learning models in healthcare settings.

Integration of Prometheus for real-time monitoring of CPU usage, memory consumption, file system activity, and network I/O ensures operational transparency, reliability, and efficient resource utilization (e.g., average CPU load of 9.37%, memory utilization of 29%).

The system bridges the gap between model development and clinical deployment by transforming static classification models into dynamic, deployable AI services with an interactive web-based interface for immediate diagnostic feedback.

Future work will focus on enhancing interpretability through explainable AI techniques and exploring adaptive resource scaling and fault recovery mechanisms to improve resilience in real-world deployments.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary classification (benign vs. malignant) using the ISIC dataset, lacking multi-class differentiation of specific skin cancer subtypes (e.g., melanoma vs. other malignancies).
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the ISIC dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to diverse populations or different imaging devices.

The paper does not report standard diagnostic metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, or AUC, relying exclusively on accuracy (92.33% for ResNet50), which may mask class imbalance issues.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was conducted to validate the performance differences between the evaluated architectures (CNN, VGG19, ResNet50).

The system was deployed and monitored on a local Proxmox testbed rather than a public cloud or real-world clinical infrastructure, limiting the assessment of scalability and fault tolerance in production environments.

The paper acknowledges that transitioning from controlled research environments to real-world healthcare settings remains a significant challenge, particularly regarding interpretability and demographic diversity.
Future work is identified as necessary to enhance interpretability through explainable AI techniques and to explore adaptive resource scaling for real-world resilience.
